water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing conditions on the Chattahoochee River are very good with ideal water temperatures and recent stocking boosting confidence. Expect steady water with low turbidity and mild weather to support active trout.
generated_text.best_window: The day 1 morning window is best due to ideal water temperature and stable light pressure, promoting trout activity in riffles, runs, seams, and structure. This period favors effective use of nymphs, midges, soft hackles, and occasional streamers.
generated_text.current_conditions: USGS reports water temperature at 57.9°F, flow at 933 cfs, gage height 2.01 ft, and turbidity at 6 NTU, indicating clear and favorable tailwater conditions.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover averages about 46% in the morning with pressure at 30.05 inHg and stable trend data unavailable. Winds are light at 5.3 mph average with no significant rain expected.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout are likely to hold in riffles, runs, seams, and structure, feeding in comfortable lanes near cover. Use presentations with clean drifts and smaller natural patterns like nymphs, midges, and stocked-trout attractors, emphasizing egg patterns, squirmy worms, and duracell flies. Fish cautiously during brighter conditions and prioritize balanced nymph approaches.
